12. EM Algorithm

Basic ideas date back at least to Fisher in 1925. Popularized by Dempster in 1977.

12.1. Motivation

Can we define a hill-climbing method that explicitly takes advantage of the special structure of the distribution if interest?

Example 12.1 (Genetic linkage problem from Dempster, Laird & Rubin's original 1977 EM paper.). Bin of R,G,B balls, probabilties are parameterized by :

(12.1)

Suppose we observe of each ball. What is the ML estimator?

(12.2)

Differentating yields a quadratic in which we can solve.

Consider an alternative strategy where we introduce a mixture: divide into 2 groups, and with and . If we know how many is in each group, solving for ML estimator becomes a linear equation!

The general principle we will try to exploit is: By introducing some fictious variables we can turn a harder optimization into an easier one.

Model: Let be the observation. Define which we refer to as complete data that is generated by a probability distribution (note the dependence on ) for some alphabet but is not fully observable. Without loss of generality13, we decompose the complete data as , where is a latent variable comprising the unobserved part of

In the lecture we did not use , so I will proceed more generally with .

Let’s consider for some deterministic14 function , possibly many-to-one, so that

(12.3)

Then

(12.4)

Taking expectation with respect to for variable to both sides15 yields

(12.5)

Apply Gibb’s inequality to conclude so that

(12.6)

This motivates the a iterative approach to increase the likelihood where it suffices to find an that maximizes since is nonnegative each step.

EM Algorithm:

Start with some initial . At step ,

  1. E-step: Compute

    (hold the current parameters fixed and compute the posterior distribution of the latent variables given the observed data)

  2. M-step: Compute

    (hold that posterior fixed and choose new parameters that maximize this expected complete-data log-likelihood)

  3. Analysis: implying

Remarks on the EM algorithm

  • A variety of what are reffered to as variational inference techniques are based on the use of optimizing lower bounds. E.g. in variational Bayes’ methods, it is the evidence lower bound (ELBO) that is optimized.
  • In practice, we can replace -step with anything that increases (generalized EM).
  • Under mild conditions (e.g. exponential family), we can show that EM converges to a stationary point of (Theorem 12.1)
  • Rate of convergence: it’s exponential and depends on choice of . As a general rule of thumb, for a given problem, the less predictable the complete data is from the observed data—the worse the exponent.
  • There’s often a natural choice for with some physical interpretation.
  • There’s a beautiful geometric interpretation that we will develop later (Section 19).

  • Pset 4 #4 goes over a Bayesian version: EM-MAP instead of EM-ML. Uses the following relation:

12.2. EM in Exponential families

We have an exponential family complete data when the complete data is from a canonical exponential family:

(12.9)

The specialization of the EM algorithm to this case is as follows

(12.10)

Solving for the ML estimate of yields:

Rewritten, this in form, this looks like:

The interpretation is that we want to maximize those two expectations (one is the conditional and one is the marginal ).

From ps4 prac 4.9, if the family is not canonical, if is invertible then we get the same result. (Kinda obvious if we just define a new canonical exponential family)

Theorem 12.1 (EM Convergence for Exponential Family). When we have exponential family complete data every fixed point of the EM algorithm is a stationary point of the likelihood function on
Proof

The condition for a fixed point is that two expectations match. It turns out this is the same condition for stationary point in an EF.

□

The convergence rate analysis can also be derived elementarily

12.3. Examples

Example 12.3 (Gaussian Mixture Model). The complete data augments with the latent component label indicating which Gaussian generated . The likelihood for a single point is just the sum below.

Example 12.4 (Logistic Model Mixture). Mixture of two logistic models, e.g. classify truck vs car while winter vs summer is confounding.. The likelihood with the component selector known is

follows a Bernoulli distribution which is exponential. Let where is the class label. Compute conditional probabilities and posteriors using Baye’s law.

Intuitively, the iterative approach is to guess a value for the , and then update the posterior distributions for (E-step), and update and assuming the posterior (M-step).

[TODO] Try these out

Mixture models are just the easiest place to see the mechanics, but EM is a general latent-variable maximum likelihood method. “Maximizing a surrogate objective where the latent variables are treated as soft labels distributed according to the current posterior”

Another example with hospital data: pset4.3
